---
title: "Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation"
---

Chapter 4 takes seriously a threat (rater dependence) that most applied MFRM studies
ignore, and builds a simulation to probe it. The revision concerns sharpening the
interpretation so the study says precisely what it shows, and no more. The relevant pages
are 76–90.

## What the dissertation does

Local independence — in Yen's (1993) formulation, as quoted on page 76 — is a
property of *residual* correlations: a pair of scores is locally independent when they are
"independent after controlling for the latent variable." In a many-facet Rasch context
that means two raters' scores on the same examinees should be uncorrelated *once examinee
ability, rater severity, and item difficulty have been accounted for*. The residual is
whatever shared signal remains after the model has explained what it can.

Chapter 4 asks whether MFRM indices are sensitive to violations of that property. The
design on page 84 selects rater pairs so that about 20% of raters belong to a correlated
pair, then "replaced one rater's scores with values that had a specific correlation with
their partner's scores" at three target levels — 0.2, 0.6, and 0.9 — describing this as
reflecting "Yen's (1993) definition of local independence as low correlations among items
after controlling for examinee ability" (pp. 84–85). The result, on page 90, is that "the
practical impact of rater dependence was minimal across conditions, suggesting that the
MFRM is robust to this type of potential local independence violation."

The logic is clean and the implementation careful. The one problem is that the manipulation
operates on a **marginal** (raw, unconditional) correlation between the two raters' scores —
not on the **residual** correlation the conclusion invokes. And because that marginal baseline
depends on the unit (total score vs. single item), even the *sign* of the dependence some
conditions impose turns out to be ambiguous.

## Why this threatens the conclusion

Start from first principles. Imagine two raters, A and B, scoring the same pool of
examinees independently — no consultation, no shared bias. Under perfect local independence
their *residual* correlation is zero by definition. Yet both are doing the same job: each
tracks examinee ability. When ability is high, both tend to award higher scores; when it is
low, both score lower. That shared pull from the latent variable creates a positive
*marginal* correlation between A and B even when they are perfectly locally independent.

Think of two thermometers hanging in the same room. Neither influences the other, but both
respond to the room temperature, so their readings move together. The correlation is not
evidence of dependence *between the thermometers* — it is evidence of the shared
environment they both measure. Rater A and rater B are two thermometers; examinee ability
is the room.

The magnitude was verified by simulating two locally-independent partial-credit MFRM raters
scoring the same examinees in R — not a re-run of TAM, but a ground-up construction.
Under perfect local independence the marginal correlation of their total scores was about
**0.79** — and of their ratings on a single shared item, about **0.43** — while a residual
correlation after partialling out ability was about **0.03**, indistinguishable from zero.
That ≈ 0.03 is a *simplified illustration* of the residual idea — the partial correlation of
the two raters' total scores given ability — rather than Yen's Q3 itself: Yen's (1993) Q3
proper correlates the *item-level residuals from the fitted model* (observed minus
model-expected for each person × item) across item or rater pairs. The illustration still
makes the point — under local independence the residual sits near zero — but a real check
would report Q3. Both marginal numbers matter; the targets land
differently against each. @fig-dependence makes
the geometry visible.

```{r}
#| label: fig-dependence
#| fig-cap: "Marginal correlation is not local dependence — and the baseline depends on the unit. **(A)** Under *perfect* local independence, two raters scoring the same examinees are already correlated at ≈ 0.79 on their 5-item *total* scores but only ≈ 0.43 on the *same single item* (the unit Yen's 'low correlations among items' names). Read against both baselines, the targets split three ways: 0.2 falls below *both* (reaching it needs *negative* residual dependence — the opposite of a violation); 0.9 sits above *both* (a genuine positive violation); and 0.6 sits *between* them — above the item baseline but below the total — so its verdict depends on scale, a genuine positive violation under the item/rating reading the 'among items' wording implies. **(B)** Under local independence the total-score marginal correlation is ≈ 0.79 but the residual correlation given ability is ≈ 0.03 — and Yen's definition is about the residual (right) bar, not the marginal (left) one."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4
#| code-summary: "Show the R code (two locally-independent raters; marginal vs residual)"
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")
set.seed(7)

I <- 5L; N_EX <- 500L; N_REP <- 400L; TARGETS <- c(0.2, 0.6, 0.9)
delta <- rnorm(I, 0, 0.5)

partial_corr <- function(x, y, z) {                  # corr(x, y | z), z quadratic
  Z <- cbind(1, z, z^2); cor(lm.fit(Z, x)$residuals, lm.fit(Z, y)$residuals)
}
two_rater_totals <- function(n) {                    # two LI raters, same examinees
  th <- rnorm(n); l1 <- rnorm(1, 0, .5); l2 <- rnorm(1, 0, .5)
  R1 <- R2 <- matrix(0, n, I)                        # per-item ratings, both raters
  for (i in seq_len(I)) { R1[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(th, l1, delta[i]))
                          R2[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(th, l2, delta[i])) }
  list(t1 = rowSums(R1), t2 = rowSums(R2), theta = th, R1 = R1, R2 = R2)
}
marg <- res <- item_base <- numeric(N_REP)
for (r in seq_len(N_REP)) { o <- two_rater_totals(N_EX)
  marg[r] <- cor(o$t1, o$t2); res[r] <- partial_corr(o$t1, o$t2, o$theta)
  item_base[r] <- mean(sapply(seq_len(I), function(i) cor(o$R1[, i], o$R2[, i]))) }
baseline <- mean(marg); sdm <- sd(marg); mres <- mean(res); sdr <- sd(res)
baseline_item <- mean(item_base)               # item/rating-level LI baseline ≈ 0.43

# Panel A: sampling distribution of the marginal correlation under LI
pA <- ggplot(data.frame(r = marg), aes(r)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.08, xmax = baseline_item, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = pal_companion[["severe"]], alpha = .07) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = baseline_item, xmax = baseline, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = pal_companion[["warn"]], alpha = .07) +
  annotate("text", x = (0.08 + baseline_item)/2, y = 21, label = "below BOTH\n→ neg. residual",
           vjust = 1, size = 2.6, colour = pal_companion[["severe"]], fontface = "italic", lineheight = .9) +
  annotate("text", x = (baseline_item + baseline)/2, y = 21, label = "between baselines\n→ scale-dependent",
           vjust = 1, size = 2.6, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "italic", lineheight = .9) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), binwidth = .01, fill = pal_companion[["none"]],
                 colour = "white", alpha = .85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline_item, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline, colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline_item - .012, y = 14, hjust = 1, size = 2.7, fontface = "bold",
           colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], label = sprintf("item baseline\nr ≈ %.2f", baseline_item)) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline + .012, y = 16, hjust = 0, size = 2.7, fontface = "bold",
           colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], label = sprintf("total baseline\nr ≈ %.2f", baseline)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = TARGETS, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = c(pal_companion[["severe"]], pal_companion[["warn"]], pal_companion[["misfit"]]), linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("label", x = .2, y = 11, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["severe"]],
           label = "r = 0.2\nbelow both\n→ neg. residual") +
  annotate("label", x = .58, y = 6, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["warn"]],
           label = "r = 0.6\nbetween → violation\nat item level") +
  annotate("label", x = .9, y = 11, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]],
           label = "r = 0.9\nabove both\n→ genuine violation") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(.2, 1, .2), limits = c(.06, .98)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .05))) +
  labs(title = "(A)  The LI baseline depends on the unit",
       subtitle = "marginal corr(rater 1, rater 2) under perfect local independence (500 examinees, 400 reps)",
       x = "marginal correlation between raters", y = "density") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 8.5))

# Panel B: marginal vs residual correlation
barB <- data.frame(type = factor(c("marginal", "residual"), levels = c("marginal", "residual")),
                   v = c(baseline, mres), lo = c(baseline - 2*sdm, mres - 2*sdr), hi = c(baseline + 2*sdm, mres + 2*sdr))
pB <- ggplot(barB, aes(type, v, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(width = .5, alpha = .85) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), width = .12, colour = pal_companion[["none"]]) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", v)), vjust = -0.6, size = 4.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("label", x = 1.5, y = .45, size = 2.85, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]],
           label = "Yen (1993) dependence\n= the residual bar,\nnot the marginal one") +
  annotate("segment", x = 1.78, xend = 1.93, y = .42, yend = mres + .07,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(.15, "cm"), type = "closed"), colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linewidth = .8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey60", linewidth = .5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(marginal = unname(pal_companion[["none"]]), residual = unname(pal_companion[["misfit"]])), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(marginal = "marginal\ncor(T1, T2)", residual = "residual\n(given ability θ)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-.05, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, .2)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  Marginal r ≈ 0.79;  residual r ≈ 0.03",
       subtitle = "shared θ drives the marginal correlation", x = NULL, y = "correlation") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 8.5))

(pA | pB) + plot_layout(widths = c(1.5, 1))
```

The targets must be read against that baseline — but first *which* baseline must be decided, because it
depends on the unit correlated. Two raters' **5-item total scores** are marginally
correlated at ≈ **0.79** under perfect local independence; their ratings on the **same single
item** are correlated at only ≈ **0.43**, because the shared-ability signal accumulates across
five items while a lone ordinal rating carries far less of it. This distinction is decisive,
because the dissertation fixes the unit at the item level: page 84 describes "replac[ing] one
rater's scores," and pages 84–85 ground the design in "Yen's (1993) definition of local
independence as low correlations *among items*." *Among items* — so the baseline that matters
is the per-item one (≈ 0.43), not the total-score one (≈ 0.79).

Read against both baselines, the three targets split three ways rather than two (Panel A):

- **0.2** sits below *both* baselines. Reaching it requires injecting *negative* residual
  correlation — suppressing the natural ability-driven co-movement, as if the raters were
  *anti*-coordinated after controlling for ability. On either unit, this is the opposite of a
  positive violation.
- **0.9** sits above *both* baselines — a genuine positive violation on either reading.
- **0.6** sits *between* the two: below the total-score baseline of 0.79, but above the
  item-level baseline of 0.43. Its verdict therefore depends on the scale the manipulation
  operated on. Read at the total-score level it would still imply mild *negative* residual
  dependence; but read at the item/rating level — the reading the dissertation's own "among
  items" wording implies — **0.6 is a genuine positive violation**, and a near-null MFRM
  response would mean the model *absorbed* a real violation rather than that none was there.

That cuts partly in the dissertation's favour. On the more defensible (item-level) reading, the 0.6 and 0.9
conditions did impose genuine positive dependence, so a null result is not *merely* an
artefact of removing co-movement. What the design still cannot settle is which scale was in
force — the manipulation's unit was never stated, and the residual correlation that actually
entered the MFRM was never measured. So a null result remains consistent with several stories
(the MFRM is genuinely robust; the 0.2 condition imposed negative dependence and could never
have flagged; or the raw-score replacement created an item-level structure the model quietly
absorbed), and the data as they stand cannot tell them apart.

There is a quieter issue too: overwriting one rater's scores with a constructed vector
changes not just the pair correlation but that rater's score distribution and severity
estimate — so the residual correlation that actually entered the MFRM (the quantity Yen's
definition is about) was never directly measured.

::: {.callout-note}
## The strongest counterargument

In the dissertation's defense, the manipulation follows Yen's (1993) "low correlations *among items*"
framing directly — it is grounded in his definition and operates at the item level, the
unit his wording names. It does genuinely move the rater-pair correlation; the conditions
are not inert. And a near-null MFRM response is not nothing — it is positive, informative
evidence about the model's robustness to the induced dependence.

And yet — granted, and this cuts partly in the dissertation's favour: under the item-level reading, the
0.6 and 0.9 conditions sit *above* the ≈ 0.43 baseline (Panel A) and so are genuine positive
violations the MFRM apparently *absorbed*, which is a point in its favour, not against it.
What the design cannot yet settle is the *cleanness* of the reading, for two stated reasons —
the manipulation's scale was never declared, and the realized residual correlation that
actually entered the MFRM was never measured. Until both are reported (ideally as Yen's Q3
per condition), a flat result stays equally consistent with "the MFRM is robust" and with
"the 0.2 condition imposed *negative* dependence that could never flag." Reporting the
realized residual per condition is the one addition that turns an ambiguous null into a
measured dose — and on the item-level reading, it likely settles the matter in the dissertation's favour.
:::

## What would fix it

::: {.callout-tip}
## Fix-it
- **Report realized correlations at both scales.** Per condition, give (a) the realized
  marginal correlation — at both the total-score and the item/rating level — and (b) the
  realized *residual* correlation, also at both levels: Yen's **Q3** — the correlation of
  *item-level residuals from the fitted MFRM* (observed minus model-expected per person ×
  item) — which is the proper diagnostic, shown beside the total-score partial correlation
  given ability (the ≈ 0.03 illustration above) for comparison. Q3 is the quantity that
  settles each condition; the total-score partial is reported only so a reader can see the
  two readings agree. This one addition lets a reviewer see where each condition truly sits.
- **Re-manipulate on the residual scale.** Induce dependence directly in residual space — a
  shared rater-pair random effect added to the log-odds (cleanest), a residual copula, a
  testlet-style MFRM pair effect, or a training-group random effect — sweeping a known
  residual correlation across, say, −0.3 to +0.5 around the LI baseline of 0.
- **Separate two phenomena.** Repeated-ratings dependence (the same script scored twice) and
  contextual dependence (a shared training cohort) have different residual structures;
  distinguishing them sharpens the contribution.
- **Compare with dependence-aware models.** Ask not only whether MFRM flags the dependence
  but whether a rater-bundle model, random-effect facet model, testlet MFRM, or HRM recovers
  parameters better when genuine positive residual dependence is present.
- **Re-scope the conclusion.** State it as "MFRM indices changed little under this specific
  score-replacement manipulation" — noting that on the item-level reading the 0.6 and 0.9
  conditions plausibly *did* impose positive dependence the model absorbed — not "robust to
  local-independence violations" in general.
:::

## What a reviewer will ask

::: {.callout-warning}
## What a reviewer will ask
"Could you report the realized residual correlation given ability in each condition, and state
the unit your target correlations were defined on? Two raters scoring the same examinees are
already marginally correlated under perfect local independence — at ≈ 0.79 on their total
scores, but only ≈ 0.43 on a single shared item. Since you cite Yen's 'low correlations *among
items*,' the item-level baseline of ≈ 0.43 looks like the relevant one — and on that reading,
0.2 sits below it (negative residual dependence, not a violation) while 0.6 and 0.9 sit *above*
it and are genuine positive violations. Could you confirm which scale the manipulation operated
on, and report the realized residual correlation per condition, so a null result can be read as
'the MFRM absorbed real positive dependence' rather than left ambiguous?"
:::

Whether the MFRM holds up when raters are *not* independent is a question the field needs
answered, and this study builds the machinery to answer it. The fix is small relative to the idea —
move the manipulation onto the residual scale, report the residual correlation actually
induced, and let the conclusion follow. Done that way, this becomes a *stronger* paper than
a clean null would have been: a study that pins down precisely how much residual dependence
the MFRM can absorb, with the dose measured on the scale Yen actually defined.
